2/14
INTRODUCTION

Introduction to Linear Models

Linear models are a class of models widely used in practice. They have been studied extensively and have roots going back over a hundred years.

1/12
SLIDE 04

Theory and Core Concepts

Linear models make a prediction using a linear function. They can only model linear relationships, which are lines in the case of a single feature. The decision tree can build a much more complex model of the data. Linear models have coefficients and an intercept.

📄 Pages 59, 70, 234
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-23. Two-moons dataset on which the decision tree will be built — Page 86
2/12
SLIDE 05

Implementation and Examples

We can apply the LogisticRegression and LinearSVC models to the forge dataset. Using a straight line to make predictions can be compared to using the KNeighborsRegressor. Linear models can be used for regression and classification.

  • LogisticRegression and LinearSVC can be applied📄 Page 70
  • KNeighborsRegressor can be used for comparison📄 Page 60
  • Linear models can be used for regression and classification📄 Page 70
📄 Pages 60, 70
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-15. Decision boundaries of a linear SVM and logistic regression on the forge — Page 71
3/12
SLIDE 06

Binning, Discretization, and Trees

Binning, discretization, linear models, and trees are related concepts. Binning involves dividing data into discrete intervals. Discretization involves converting continuous data into discrete data. Linear models can be used with binned or discretized data.

  • Binning involves dividing data into intervals📄 Page 234
  • Discretization involves converting continuous to discrete data📄 Page 234
  • Linear models can be used with binned or discretized data📄 Page 237
📄 Pages 234, 237
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-1. Comparing linear regression and a decision tree on the wave dataset — Page 235
4/12
SLIDE 07

Binning, Discretization, and Trees Examples

We can build a new linear regression model and a new decision tree model on one-hot-encoded data. The result is visible when using the np.digitize function. Binning can be used to prepare data for linear models.

  • New linear regression model can be built on one-hot-encoded data📄 Page 237
  • np.digitize function can be used for binning📄 Page 235
  • Binning can prepare data for linear models📄 Page 237
📄 Pages 235, 237
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-2. Comparing linear regression and decision tree regression on binned features — Page 237
5/12
SLIDE 08

Univariate Nonlinear Transformations

Univariate nonlinear transformations can help linear models for regression. Adding squared or cubed features can help. Other transformations can also be used, such as the Poisson distribution.

  • Univariate nonlinear transformations can help linear models📄 Page 246
  • Adding squared or cubed features can help📄 Page 246
  • Poisson distribution can be used for transformations📄 Page 248
📄 Pages 246, 248
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-3. Linear regression using binned features and a single global slope — Page 239
6/12
SLIDE 09

Univariate Nonlinear Transformations Examples

Finding the transformation that works best for each combination of dataset and model is somewhat of an art. In this example, all features had the same properties. The transformation can help improve the model's performance.

  • Finding the best transformation is an art📄 Page 249
  • All features had the same properties in this example📄 Page 249
  • Transformation can improve model's performance📄 Page 249
📄 Page 249
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 4-8. Histogram of feature values for X[0] after logarithmic transformation — Page 249
7/12
SLIDE 10

Univariate Nonlinear Transformations Parameters

The parameters of univariate nonlinear transformations can affect the model's performance. The choice of transformation can depend on the dataset and the model. The Poisson distribution can be used for transformations, but it is a very crude approximation.

  • Parameters of transformations can affect performance📄 Page 246
  • Choice of transformation depends on dataset and model📄 Page 246
  • Poisson distribution is a crude approximation📄 Page 249
📄 Pages 246, 249
8/12
SLIDE 11

Univariate Statistics

Univariate statistics can be used to analyze data before applying linear models. Binning, polynomials, and interactions can have a huge influence on how models perform on a given dataset. Automatic feature selection can be used to select the most important features.

  • Univariate statistics can analyze data before linear models📄 Page 250
  • Binning, polynomials, and interactions can affect model performance📄 Page 250
  • Automatic feature selection can select important features📄 Page 250
📄 Page 250
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 7-4. Most important features when using unigrams, bigrams, and trigrams with — Page 357
9/12
SLIDE 12

Univariate Statistics Examples

We can use the cancer dataset to demonstrate univariate statistics. We add some noninformative noise features to the data and expect the feature selection to identify the noninformative features and remove them.

  • Cancer dataset can be used for demonstration📄 Page 251
  • Noninformative noise features can be added to data📄 Page 251
  • Feature selection can identify noninformative features📄 Page 251
📄 Page 251
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-43. Feature ranges for the Breast Cancer dataset (note that the y axis has a log‐ — Page 116
10/12
SLIDE 13

Model-Based Feature Selection

Model-based feature selection can be used to select the most important features for a linear model. Decision trees and decision tree-based models provide a feature_importances_ attribute, which can be used to capture feature importances.

  • Model-based feature selection can select important features📄 Page 253
  • Decision trees provide a feature_importances_ attribute📄 Page 253
  • Feature importances can be captured using feature_importances_📄 Page 253
📄 Page 253
🔍 Click to enlarge
Figure 2-28. Feature importances computed from a decision tree learned on the Breast — Page 92
11/12
✦

Summary of Linear Models

Linear models are a class of models that can be used for regression and classification. They have been studied extensively and have roots going back over a hundred years. Univariate nonlinear transformations and model-based feature selection can be used to improve the performance of linear models.

Linear models
12/12
